content.problemStatement:
Divyam is teaching his student Amit about palindromes. A palindrome is a word, phrase, number, or other sequence of characters which reads the same backward or forward. For example, the string “abcba” is a palindrome. After teaching about it, Divyam gave a puzzle for Amit to solve:




Given a string **S**. Find the number of positions in **S** where we can insert a character to convert it into a palindrome. Character can be inserted in the beginning or the end, so there will be **(N+1)** possible positions for the insertion in a string having original length **N**. For example, if **S** = “aaa”, then there are four positions where we can insert a character so that the converted string is a palindrome. Converted palindromes are “**a**aaa”, “a**a**aa”, “aa**a**a” and “aaa**a**”. Inserted character is shown in boldface.



Amit finds the puzzle too difficult. Can you help him in solving it?
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a non‑empty string S consisting of lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the number of positions (0 … |S|) where inserting exactly one character can make the resulting string a palindrome.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For "aaa" every of the 4 possible insertion positions yields a palindrome. For "aca" only inserting before the first character or after the last character works.
No single insertion can turn "abc" into a palindrome.
The string is already a palindrome, therefore all N+1 = 5 positions are valid.

solution.approach: For a string S of length N we examine the two ends with two pointers l and r. While S[l]==S[r] we move l++ and r--. If the whole string is a palindrome, any of the N+1 insertion positions works, so answer = N+1. Otherwise we have the first mismatching pair (l,r). To fix the string by inserting one character we have only two possibilities: insert a character equal to S[r] just before position l (i.e., at index l) – this is feasible iff the substring S[l+1…r] is already a palindrome; or insert a character equal to S[l] just after position r (i.e., at index r+1) – feasible iff the substring S[l…r-1] is a palindrome. We check both substrings with a simple two‑pointer scan. The answer is the count of feasible possibilities (0, 1 or 2). The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N = |S|
